channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.thesixthaxis.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.thesixthaxis.com

item.title: MIO: Memories In Orbit Patch 2 is now live bringing more fixes
item.content:encoded:
Developer Douze Dixièmes has released patch 2 for its metroidvania MIO: Memories In Orbit, with this patch addressing more issues that needed fixing. That includes areas where you could be hard locked from progressing further for not having the right unlocks, and soft locks where some characters may just disappear. Unsafe zones have also been added so it is clearer where players may get caught in a death loop.

MIO: Memories In Orbit Patch 2 update notes

Hard locks

  • Death on Anra after opening the door, if the player quits the game after Halyn’s cutscene without taking the checkpoint.
  • Accessing the Vault area without the Flowing Striders and then taking the checkpoint.

Soft locks / can be resolved by returning to the menu

  • Reaching the big fly arena without the hook.
  • Reaching certain platforms in the flashbacks.
  • Complete disappearance of Ancile & Targa, leaving the player stuck in the room.

Other fixes included

  • Fixed Spanish localization.
  • SFX fixes.
  • Credits update.
  • Invisible killzone in Promenade.
  • Added the holographic planet in the true ending.
  • Various fixes: flowing steps, Plums disappearing during Saw Long chase, elevators, pearls in The Pit, incorrect name for Mel at the end, Flora’s corpse, Goliath disappearing, etc.

QoL improvements and balancing

  • Elevator automatically unlocked after the first encounter with Halyn.
  • Added “unsafe” zones to prevent death loops (avoid blocking respawns).
  • Balance changes: adjustment to the perfect Sharpened Hairpin, adjustments to negative modifiers.
  • Keyboard fixes for Flowing Striders.
  • Promenade’s overseer moved.

In our MIO: Memories In Orbit review, I said, “MIO: Memories In Orbit is an early contender for one of the most memorable games of 2026. It is an incredibly challenging game to play, with combat being a highlight, even if some platforming section can be frustrating. It looks fantastic, though, and the story is well told. MIO: Memories In Orbit draws you in, and does not let go easily. It is hard game but the journey is worth experiencing.”

Source: Steam



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/12/mio-memories-in-orbit-patch-2-is-now-live-bringing-more-fixes/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: DEFECT story trailer released as NCSoft joins development
item.content:encoded:
Back in October, emptyvessel released a first proper look at its 4v4v4v4 cyberpunk styled first person shooter DEFECT, in which The System’s Police Force, Rogue Police, or and different gangs all try to complete their own objectives while sabotaging the other teams. Now, emptyvessel has released the first story teaser for DEFECT’s single player mode.

In the description we get a small idea of what to expect with emptyvessel saying, “You play a System Control Officer who has been marked deficient by The System, a godlike artificial intelligence that governs the world.” The teaser tells us that this yet unnamed System Control Officer is trying to survive in a world where he is no longer seen as useful after providing his services to The System. In addition, emptyvessel also announced that NCSoft has joined as a partner to help develop the DEFECT’s single player mode.

A release date is yet to be confirmed for the game, with PC currently confirmed as the only platform with Steam Deck compatibility in the works. If you want to check out DEFECT ahead of its release you can sign up to a list to join future playtests, though these are yet to be dated.

Source: Steam



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/12/defect-story-trailer-released-as-ncsoft-joins-development/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Moonsigil Atlas is a deckbuilding roguelike where shapes not energy determine your tactics
item.content:encoded:
Publisher Two Sails Interactive along with developer Snake Tower Games have revealed that the deckbuilding roguelike Moonsigil Atlas will be releasing this June on Steam, and a demo is now available to try out. What makes Moonsigil Atlas different from other deckbuilders is that card shapes, not energy, determine what you can lay down on the board.

In Moonsigil Atlas players can choose between three characters who have their own styles of play, with 200 cards spread between them. These cards can be upgraded with runes and keywords, as well as having their shapes changed to better fit your own tactical approach. As Moonsigil Atlas is a roguelike you will go through many runs to unlock new cards and upgrades, as well as finding new routes to get to the Astral Titans to take them down.

At the moment, it appears that Moonsigil Atlas will only be available on PC.

Source: Press Release



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/12/moonsigil-atlas-is-a-deckbuilding-roguelike-where-shapes-not-energy-determine-your-tactics/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Fortnite Save the World will be free to play from April 16th
item.content:encoded:
Epic Games has finally confirmed the rumours around Fortnite Save The World going free to play, stating that the PvE mode will be free from April 16th. While Fortnite Battle Royale dominates the market, it was Save The World that came first as a paid experience in which players team up to build forts and fight back against monsters.

Those who paid for Fortnite Save The World will be given the following rewards on April 16th when free to play starts:

  • 1x Hero Supercharger – Promote a Hero beyond Level 50.
  • 1x Weapon Supercharger – Promote a weapon beyond Level 50.
  • 1x Trap Supercharger – Promote a Trap beyond Level 50.
  • 5x Survivor Supercharger – Promote Survivors beyond Level 50.
  • 1x Hero Recruitment Voucher – Recruit a Hero of your choice.
  • 1x Weapon Research Voucher – Unlock a weapon of your choice.
  • 10000 Gold – Spend in the Event Store (to be renamed “the Exchange”) for valuable items.

All players, old and new, can sign up for pre-registration now to get rewards as long as milestones are met. The first reward will unlock at 300,000 registrations and that seems to be Kyle’s Construction Spray. The second reward, unlocked at 700,000 registrations, is the Save The World Jess Banner, and the final reward, unlocked at 1,000,000 registrations is the Snowstrike Hero. This character has the following perks:

  • Standard Perk: North Star
    • Throwing Stars will slow enemies for 4 seconds instead of dealing damage. Slowed-down enemies will explode for 167 base water damage if they receive melee damage or damage from another North Star explosion.
  • Commander Perk: North Star+
    • Throwing Stars will freeze enemies for 4 seconds instead of dealing damage. Frozen enemies will explode for 167 base water damage if they receive melee damage or damage from another North Star explosion.

Fortnite Save The World will be playable on PS5, PS4, Xbox Series XS, Xbox One, Xbox Cloud Gaming, Switch 2, PC, GeForce Now, and Amazon Luna. It will not be available on smartphones, tablets, or the original Switch.

Source: Epic Games



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/12/fortnite-save-the-world-will-be-free-to-play-from-april-16th/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Laysara: Summit Kingdom Review
item.content:encoded:
City builders are one of my favourite types of game. Building an idyllic little town, putting together efficient production lines, and planning where more of them will go later is an addictive and immensely satisfying loop. Of course, mess it up and you’ll find yourself in a budgetary black hole, creating a death spiral as your town slowly perishes. Laysara: Summit Kingdom is one such game, but this time, you’re building on the side of a mountain, planning for avalanches, and building bridges to access other parts of the mountain.

The kingdom of Laysara is awash with a thick fog that blankets the entire region. The obvious solution, then, is to literally rise above it and build your towns on the mountains, so that’s what you set out to do with three bickering advisors at your side. One is a monk, one is nice and friendly, whilst the last and least of the trio is obviously focused only on dangers and profit. They’ll guide you through a very necessary tutorial, because whilst the basics here are what you might expect, there are a few crucial differences.

For one thing, you’ll need to set up supply chains yourself, which means you need to tell newly constructed building where it will send its production and supplies, or it will just sit there doing nothing. You’ll also need a good amount of bridges to connect up the buildable slopes of the mountain you’re on, and shafts – which are basically lifts – to get higher up the mountain. This is important, as certain resources will grow more efficiently at certain altitudes, not to mention the preferences of the different worker types.

Monks prefer higher altitudes, while there are also lowlanders, who will work farms and other more menial jobs, and artisans, who do skilled work like blacksmithing. You’ll grow familiar with these groups, as meeting their needs allows you to upgrade their housing, increasing the amount of people that live there. Unsurprisingly, space is a bit hard to find when you’re building on the side of a mountain, and upgrading your housing saves a lot of it. To please them you’ll have to have a look at what they want, obviously, then build it. It can be types of food, access to tools, baths, places of worship, some of which can be really tricky to accomplish depending on the mountain you’re working with.

Technically speaking, if you don’t have enough workers to fulfil your needs they’ll be imported, but at a huge cost to you. Seriously, building a mine, then a forge, then a building to distribute the goods, could take you from +500 income down to the negatives if you have to import all the workers.

At least you don’t have to worry about your workers dying in battle as there is no combat in this game. It’s pure building, and the enemy is the elements. More specifically, it’s avalanches, which can decimate large parts of your town if you don’t plan ahead. This isn’t an “I’ll cross that bridge when I get there” kind of problem, it’s a “This will destroy the whole bridge if I don’t plan first” thing. You can use foresters to create natural tree barriers, both for a resource and to block weaker avalanches, but they won’t stop the stronger ones on their own. Instead you’ll need another layer of walls to stop the avalanche after it’s been weakened by the tree barrier. It’s a simple enough system, but it works well enough.

As your town gets more advanced there are a variety of quite fiddly, but very effective and satisfying tools. Your buildings can only send their supply so far, so you can use carrier posts to not only enhance that range, but also distribute a resource to multiple different buildings. For example, a mine that delivers to a blacksmith, that delivers to a distributor is inefficient, as the mine provides more copper than the smith can use. If you deliver it to a carrier post, you can build additional mines and have the carrier post distribute them automatically.

The range on buildings on carrier posts is quite short, but you can send stuff through a cart post to basically do the same thing throughout most of the island. These are incredibly useful tools that really allow you to set up pretty complex chains, which is really enjoyable provided the slight fiddliness doesn’t bother you. Playing on console, that’s accentuated by the controls as well. For example, in the tutorial you’re told you can connect buildings for a supply chain using triangle on the DualSense, but this button also opens the floating campaign mission window, so doesn’t work most of the time. Thankfully, you can do it from the building’s menu as well. There can be a little awkwardness with how the menus work on a controller as well, but as there isn’t a huge amount of space to comb over and the cursor speed isn’t too high, so it’s not the biggest problem.

Whilst you’re doing all of this, the game just looks beautiful. In addition to the sky-scraping setting, it’s bright and colourful and every little thing you add just somehow fits into the idyllic little mountain village you’ve built. It’s a great looking game, even the avalanches look good, though you’ll be too distracted to notice if you’ve forgotten to plan for them.

There aren’t too many negatives here really. It’s a bit light on the strategy really, there are definitely more complex city builders out there, so if that’s what you’re looking for then you might be a little underwhelmed by the difficulty on offer. If you’re playing on console there are a few control issues you might expect, but less so than in many other similar games. Other than that, it can be a little difficult to find what you’re looking for in the building menu, but you adjust to that pretty quickly.



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/12/laysara-summit-kingdom-review/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
